2004-07-01.title: 60 Bosnian Serbs Dismissed For Aid to War Crimes Figure
2004-07-01.abstract: Sixty Bosnian Serb politicians and officials, including interior minister and speaker of Parliament, are dismissed for their failure to arrest region's leading war crimes suspect Dr Radovan Karadzic; Lord Ashdown, international high representative for Bosnia, also freezes bank accounts of main Bosnian Serb party and several major companies; list of those removed from office includes mayors, police commanders and members of Parliament; officials in Ashdown's office say cost of continuing to support Karadzic will be much higher now that funds have been frozen and jobs have been lost (M)

2004-07-01.title: Daily Vitamin Can Thwart AIDS Progress, Study Says
2004-07-01.abstract: Eight-year study by Harvard researchers finds that daily vitamin pill can delay progress of AIDS in HIV-infected women; researchers say vitamins are not a cure or substitute for antiretroviral therapy, but for malnourished women in Africa or Asia with little hope of getting better drugs, vitamins are cheap, safe way of giving them extra months of life and little less misery before they die; study is published in The New England Journal of Medicine; World Health Orgn says six million people in poor countries are already sick enough to need antiretroviral drugs, but only 400,000 are getting them; efforts to increase that number are hindered by high drug prices, fights over patents, lack of money from donors, reluctance by African leaders to admit their nations have epidemics and inability of shattered health care systems to muster enough doctors, nurses and laboratories to safely deliver drugs (M)

2004-07-01.title: Thousands of Protesters in Hong Kong Take Aim at Beijing
2004-07-01.abstract: Hundreds of thousands showed a new boldness in denouncing mainland China for banning general elections.

2004-07-01.title: 1 in 6 Iraq Veterans Is Found to Have Stress-Related Disorder
2004-07-01.abstract: About one in six soldiers returning from war in Iraq shows signs of post-traumatic stress disorder or other emotional difficulties; study published in New England Journal of Medicine is first to examine mental health of troops returning from Iraq; researchers surveyed more than 6,000 soldiers in months before and after service in Iraq and in Afghanistan, where lower levels of psychiatric problems are found among troops; 17 percent of those who fought in Iraq report major depression, severe anxiety or post-traumatic stress disorder, compared with 11 percent of troops who served in Afghanistan; rates are slightly higher than those found amnong soldiers in Persian Gulf war, but lower than rates in Vietnam veterans; however, mental health studies of soldiers in those earlier conflicts were carried out years after troops returned home; graph (M)

2004-07-01.title: Israel's High Court: 'No Security Without Law'
2004-07-01.abstract: Excerpt from Israeli Supreme Court decision on barrier being built between Israel and West Bank (M)

2004-07-02.title: . . . And Other Italian Engines Are Stilled by Bureaucracy
2004-07-02.abstract: Italian authorities begin enforcing new rules requiring people of ages 14 to 17 to secure so-called mini-driving license to take to roads on scooters, which are ubiquitous in country; authorities in most cities report that number of people taking test to get license is only small fraction of number supposed to take test; it is not clear how many illicit scooter-riders have been stopped by police officers empowered to impose fines and to confiscate offenders' motorcycles; photo (M)

2004-07-02.title: A Sense of Arab Humiliation, but a Warning to Despotic Rulers
2004-07-02.abstract: Governments across Middle East largely keep silent about extraordinary appearance of Arab leader--Saddam Hussein--in the dock; some in Arab countries outside Iraq are pleased to see dictator being arraigned, but many others say court appearance merely extends sense of Arab humiliation that grew out of American invasion; foreign ministers in Egypt and Jordan refuse to comment; sole official voice supporting trial comes from Kuwait, where American invasion of Iraq is seen as revenge for Hussein's invasion of Kuwait in 1990; photo (M)

2004-07-02.title: Armani stays 70 years young
2004-07-02.abstract: MILAN—  'Night and Day," crooned the music as Giorgio Armani ended the final collection of his 69th year by paying homage to his workers. They were lined up, all in white, behind a gauze backcloth as Armani, who turns 70 this month, bounded out to take his bow.

2004-07-02.title: Is the draft coming back?American rumor mill thinks so
2004-07-02.abstract: WASHINGTON:—  Amid fears of open-ended military engagements in Iraq and Afghanistan, and ever more urgent-sounding indications of how stretched U.S. forces are around the globe, rumors that the military draft might be reinstated have surged across the United States, moving in recent months from obscure Internet sites into the mainstream media.

2004-07-02.title: New Swell of Insurgent Violence Rolls into Baghdad
2004-07-02.abstract: A botched rocket attack on two hotels used by foreigners paralyzed the city center.

2004-07-03.title: China Is Filtering Phone Text Messages to Regulate Criticism
2004-07-03.abstract: China begins filtering billions of telephone text messages to ensure that people do not use communications tool to undermine one-party rule; campaign comes after text messages sent between China's nearly 300 million mobile phone users helped to expose national cover-up of SARS epidemic in 2003; photo (M)

2004-07-03.title: As Terror Attacks Return, Reasons Haunt Pakistanis
2004-07-03.abstract: Police trainee Akbar Niazi on May 7 blew himself up inside Shiite Muslim mosque, killing 25 people in attack that marked beginning of six weeks of religious and political violence in Karachi, Pakistan; attacks have left 70 people dead, 200 wounded and Karachi destabilized; have also raised questions about effectiveness of Pres Pervez Musharraf's government, number of young Pakistanis joining militant groups and possible infiltration of Pakistan's army and police forces; photos (M)

2004-07-03.title: Delays in Athens Raise Concern On Olympic Security Readiness
2004-07-03.abstract: Intelligence officials who fear terrorism at Athens Olympics say delays in completing security apparatus and athletic facilities have left too little time to fully test systems meant to detect or respond to attack; say security command center will not be operating completely until mid-July; say Science Applications International Corp, US-led consortium building system, has provided defective software and failed to complete work on command center by May 28 deadline; map; photos (M)

2004-07-03.title: Group Tied to Al Qaeda Warns Europe of Attacks in Near Future
2004-07-03.abstract: Abu Hafs al-Masri Brigades, obscure group claiming ties to Al Qaeda, says terrorist attacks will begin in Europe after 'truce' offered by Osama bin Laden earlier in year expires in two weeks; terrorism experts say threat is hoax (M)

2004-07-03.title: Capture of Hussein Aides Spurred U.S. Interrogators
2004-07-03.abstract: US military jailers at Abu Ghraib prison received important batch of prisoners within days after Saddam Hussein's capture in Dec 2003; they were bodyguards and other loyalists who tended to Hussein in his final weeks on run; military intelligence soldiers at prison say capture of bodyguards led to all-out push for information about Hussein's close supporters who were suspected of plotting against US occupation; say interrogators were given greater latitude to use tactics on new prisoners that had previously required signed approval of senior officers; say tactics included sleep and food deprivation, extended isolation and use of menacing dogs; worst known abuses at Abu Ghraib occurred before Hussein's capture, and involved members of military police who have said their actions were encouraged by prison officials (M)

2004-07-04.title: Indonesians Prepare to Vote on Monday
2004-07-04.abstract: JAKARTA, Indonesia July 4 — As Indonesians prepare to vote on Monday in the first direct presidential elections since the fall of the Suharto regime, the biggest question is whether the front-runner, a former general, can muster the more than 50 percent of the vote he will need to avoid a runoff.

2004-07-04.title: Iraqi Prime Minister Says al-Sadr Might Get Amnesty
Militant Iraqi Group Says It Beheaded a Kidnapped American Marine
2004-07-04.abstract: Abdullah al-Hassan bin-Mahmoud, leader of Ansar al-Sunna militant group, posts message on Internet, claiming to have beheaded Marine Cpl Wassef Ali Hassoun; it is unclear whether this is true, or what relationship is between Ansar al-Sunna and 20th Revolution Regiment which earlier released video claiming to have kidnapped Hassoun; Ansar al-Sunna includes some of most violent elements from Ansar al-Islam militant group of northern Iraq; photo (M)

2004-07-04.title: Indonesians Hope a Real Election on Monday Will Bring Real Change
2004-07-04.abstract: Gen Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono is front-runner going into first round of Indonesian presidential elections on July 5; polls give him 43.5 percent of vote, but not 50 percent needed to avoid run-off election; closest rival Gen Wiranto has only 14.2 percent of vote; incumbent Pres Megawati Sukarnoputri has little support; Yudhoyono's supporters hope that he will bring change and stability without pushing religious agenda; photos; map (M)

2004-07-04.title: OFFICIALS DETAIL A DETAINEE DEAL BY 3 COUNTRIES
2004-07-04.abstract: American and British officials say United States returned five terrorism suspects to Saudi Arabia from Guantanamo Bay, Cuba, in May 2003 as part of three-way deal intended to satisfy important allies in invasion of Iraq; say Saudi officials later freed five Britons and two others convicted of terrorist attacks in Saudi Arabia as part of deal; British diplomats believe men were tortured by Saudis into false confessions; Pentagon, Central Intelligence Agency and Justice Department officials reportedly opposed transfer of Saudis initially, questioning whether some detainees were too dangerous and whether Saudis would keep promises to imprison them; releases were public relations coups for Saudi Arabia and Britain, which faced domestic criticism of their roles in Iraq war; National Security Council denies Saudis were transferred in return for Britons; Saudi officials give contradictory accounts of whereabouts of five and of their legal status; photos (M)

2004-07-05.title: Ex-General Leads Vote in Indonesia
2004-07-05.abstract: Former Gen. Bambang Susilo Yudhoyono was likely to win but will probably face a run-off in September.

2004-07-05.title: U.S. Authorizes Families to Leave Bahrain
2004-07-05.abstract: State Department authorizes voluntary departure of its employees' families and nonemergency personnel at US Embassy in Bahrain after warning that terrorist attacks on Americans might be imminent; action follows order by Pentagon to pull out families of defense personnel and other employees at major base that supports US Navy's Fifth Fleet; warnings come two weeks after authorities in Bahrain arrest, then release, six men who were initially suspected of planning attacks; photo (M)

2004-07-05.title: Communists' Slide Weakens Checks on Putin's Power
2004-07-05.abstract: Russia's Communist Party convenes meeting, but internal strife and outmaneuvering by Pres Vladimir Putin leave party at its weakest point since its return in 1993 after two-year ban; slide toward irrelevance signifies ascendancy of Putin and what analysts describe as attendant risks of that rise; turnabout has been swift, with number of seats in 450-seat Duma dropping from 113 to 51 in last election; breakaway faction meets separately and claims to have rounded up quorum, but Zyuganov refuses to recognize revolt; political chaos mirrors underlying ideological confusion; photos (M)

2004-07-05.title: Tables Turned, a Victim Sees Persecutor in Court
2004-07-05.abstract: Tables are turned in Iraq as some of those who were imprisoned and tortured during Saddam Hussein regime, or who fled country, now hold seats of power and air their charges against him in court; crowds around nation are transfixed by television replays of Hussein's exchanges with judge; photo (M)

2004-07-05.title: IRAQ UPDATE
2004-07-05.abstract: Recap of latest activities in Iraq; map (M)

2004-07-06.title: For some, French settlement is $3,000 : For Nazi victims, a low payout rate from banks
2004-07-06.abstract: NEW YORK:—  When Simon Jeruchim was 12, the French police raided and sealed his family's apartment in Montreuil, on the outskirts of Paris.

2004-07-06.title: Goryeong Journal; A Young Love Defies Both Distance and the Decades
2004-07-06.abstract: Love story involving couple in Goryeong, South Korea, has happy ending after 58 years; man left woman in 1943 to pursue riches by working in Japanese-ruled Sakhalin Island, intending to stay two years; he and other Koreans were prevented from leaving when Soviets took over island; in 2001, he did come home and was reunited with his love; now she, age 80, is hospitalized and he, age 82, visits daily to tend to her; photo; map (M)

2004-07-06.title: Goodbye, butterfly
2004-07-06.abstract: When 21-year-old Izumi Mori , in a wedding gown, closes her grandmother's show on Wednesday, it will be the end of a 50-year love affair with fashion for Hanae Mori.

2004-07-06.title: Six Palestinians and Israeli Killed in Clashes
2004-07-06.abstract: The Palestinian dead included four militants, but the ISraeli military also acknowledged the deaths of "two innocent Palestinians."

2004-07-06.title: 15 Miles Offshore, Safeguarding Iraq's Oil Lifeline
2004-07-06.abstract: Armada of American, British and Australian warships is circling Iraq's offshore oil depots to prevent threat of waterborne suicide attackers; Iraqi oil flows through rattling pipelines to depots; vulnerable site is crucial to country's economic future; in April, attackers in three boats sped toward depots and blew themselves up, killing themselves, two American sailors and coast guardsman but not causing extensive damage to terminals; but it served notice that depots are under threat by someone with deep knowledge of Iraqi oil industry; Prime Min Iyad Allawi says sabotage attacks on pipelines crisscrossing desert mainland has already cost nation $200 million in lost revenue; broken pipelines can be fixed in few days, but any severe damage to giant, isolated oil terminals could be devastating to country counting on its oil wealth as best hope for stable future; photos; map (M)

2004-07-07.title: U.N. Report Shows Concern Over Rise of H.I.V. in Asia
2004-07-07.abstract: UN reports that proportion of world's new HIV infections occurring in Asia has risen sharply in past two years; nearly 1.2 million of estimated 4.8 new infections in 2003 occurred in Asia; photo; graph; rate of new infection worldwide was highest ever (M)

2004-07-07.title: Carter, in Jakarta, Praises the Election
2004-07-07.abstract: Former Pres Jimmy Carter, in Jakarta, declares himself satisfied with conduct of Indonesian election (S)

2004-07-07.title: Suit Claims Arab Bank Aided Militants' Relatives
2004-07-07.abstract: Relatives of Americans killed in attacks in Israel bring suit in federal court in Brooklyn, charging Arab Bank of Jordan with aiding terrorism by paying life insurance to families of Palestinian suicide bombers; suit says bank channeled funds from Saudi government organization to Hamas and Islamic Jihad, paying $5,316 to families (S)

2004-07-07.title: New Law in Iraq Gives Premier Martial Powers to Fight Uprising
2004-07-07.abstract: Iraqi Prime Min Iyad Allawi signs into law broad martial powers enabling him to impose curfews, ban groups considered seditious and order detentions of people deemed security risks; says forceful response to tenacious insurgency was written with input from lawyers and ministers of justice and human rights, interview; calls on Iraqis to take responsibility to enforce law and order and fight terrorists; photo; draft obtained by The New York Times quoted (M)

2004-07-08.title: U.S. Starts Drawing Plans To Cut Its Troops in Iraq
2004-07-08.abstract: Pentagon begins long-range planning on how to reduce number of American troops in Iraq, now that interim Iraqi government is in place; continued American presence is sore spot for new Iraqi government as it seeks to establish credibility with Iraqi people; senior military officials say that how long American forces stay in Iraq and in what numbers will be driven by security conditions and how quickly Iraqi security forces establish themselves; photo (M)

2004-07-08.title: Five U.S. Soldiers Are Killed in Iraq as Mortars Hit Base
2004-07-08.abstract: Also, the abduction of a Filipino led the president of the Philippines to order citizens to stop traveling to Iraq in search of work.

2004-07-08.title: African Leaders Failing Zimbabwe, Prelate Says
2004-07-08.abstract: Archbishop Pius Ncube, top Roman Catholic official in Zimbabe, accuses Africa's heads of state of complicity in prolonging repression in Zimbabwe, days after African Union shelves official report condemning country's human-rights conduct; Ncube is one of most visible critics of autocratic government of Pres Robert Mugabe; he and other Zimbabwean religious leaders forecast new violence--and new exodus of refugees from Zimbabwe--unless African leaders press Mugabe to embrace democratic reforms; Ncube is especially critical of South African Pres Thabo Mbeki, who has urged tolerance of Mugabe's government as part of campaign to promote peaceful change in Zimbabwe (M)

2004-07-08.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia, Georgia: Tension Over Ossetia
2004-07-08.abstract: Russia and Georgia quarrel over arms intercepted in South Ossetia, Georgian separatist region seeking to become part of Russia; Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov holds that weapons shipment was approved by regional peacekeeping coalition; map (S)

2004-07-09.title: Missing Marine Turns Up at Embassy In Beirut, but the Mystery Only Deepens
2004-07-09.abstract: Wassef Ali Hassoun, Lebanese-American marine reported missing in Iraq three weeks ago, turns up at American Embassy in Beirut, Lebanon, accompanied by relatives; circumstances surrounding his disappearance remain murky; his sudden resurfacing--and shootout earlier in day near his relatives' home in Tripoli--add to mystery; defense officials say they have no details on how he got from his unit in Iraq to Beirut; he will be flown to American military hospital in Germany for medical and psychological examinations and to be questioned by investigators; photos (M)

2004-07-09.title: Khasab Journal; Sightseeing in Oman? You Mustn't Miss the Smugglers
2004-07-09.abstract: Smuggling goods to and from Iran is thought to represent over half the economy in Musandam peninsula in northern Oman; trade is illegal in Iran, which closed its borders after 1979 revolution, but legal in Oman, which means Omani government collects taxes and grants de facto visas to Iranians, as long as they stay in port and leave before nightfall; between 50 and 80 boats currently make crossing of Strait of Hormuz each day, but smugglers complain that their business is suffering since Iran began signing deals with international companies; photo; map (M)

2004-07-09.title: C.I.A. Director Again Disputes Hijacker's Iraqi Contact
2004-07-09.abstract: George J Tenet, departing director of central intelligence, sends letter to Congress stating that CIA is 'increasingly skeptical' that Sept 11 hijacker Mohamed Atta met Iraqi intelligence officer in Prague in Apr 2001, event Vice Pres Dick Cheney says might have taken place; Tenet says it is unlikely Atta would have taken such a risk when Sept 11 plot was well under way; within Bush administration, Cheney has been most vigorous proponent of theory that Iraq and Al Qaeda had collaborative relationship before Sept 11 attacks, even after presidential commission investigating attacks concluded that there is no evidence of such a relationship; photo (M)

2004-07-09.title: World Briefing | Europe: Austria: A New President
2004-07-09.abstract: Heinz Fischer is sworn in as Austrian president two days after death of predecessor Thomas Klestil (S)

2004-07-09.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Reporter Stabbed
2004-07-09.abstract: Farid Omar, Moscow correspondent for Radio Free Europe-Radio Liberty, is in critical condition after being stabbed outside his apartment; Reporters Without Borders calls on Russia's interior minister to investigate motive for attack (S)

2004-07-10.title: The Senators' Views and Excerpts from the Report on Iraq Assessments
2004-07-10.abstract: Excerpts from statements by Senate Select Intelligence Committee chairman Pat Roberts and vice chairman John D Rockefeller IV and text of committee's report on intelligence community's prewar assessments on Iraq; photo (L)

2004-07-10.title: World Briefing | The Environment: Gender Identity Problems In Britain
2004-07-10.abstract: British Environment Agency publishes findings of survey that shows 500 out of 1,500 male fish in British rivers show female characteristics believed to be caused by female hormones polluting water (S)

2004-07-10.title: British Opponents of Iraq War Look to U.S. Report as Evidence
2004-07-10.abstract: British opponents of pro-American stance of Prime Min Tony Blair, whose support for war against Iraq has made him increasingly unpopular, say Senate Intelligence Committee's report is further evidence that Britain entered war on false premises (M)

2004-07-10.title: In Words of World Court, a Wall That Is 'Contrary to International Law'
2004-07-10.abstract: Excerpts from nonbinding decision by International Court of Justice, which concluded that Israel's construction of barrier in West Bank is illegal (M)

2004-07-10.title: U.S. Investigative Journalist Is Shot to Death in Russia
2004-07-10.abstract: Paul Klebnikov, editor in chief of Forbes Russia magazine who had written about connections of politics, business and crime in Russia throughout its post-Soviet reorganization, is shot to death on Moscow street; photo (M)

2004-07-11.title: Tenet's Leadership, His Pride, Faces Attack From Senate Panel as He Leaves C.I.A.
2004-07-11.abstract: CIA Dir George J Tenet leaves office today under shadow of Senate Intelligence Committee report that criticizes him for failure of leadership, holding him responsible for failing to halt train of misjudgments, exaggerations and unquestioned assumptions that led to prewar declaration that Iraq possessed illicit weapons; report cites agency's 'broken corporate culture and poor management' and unwillingness to take risks to develop sources and insert operations officers; is harshest indictment yet among divided assessments of Tenet's tenure; photo (M)

2004-07-11.title: H.I.V. Goal Still Possible, U.N. Health Unit Says
2004-07-11.abstract: World Health Organization says it can still meet program goal to give antiretroviral therapy to three million people infected with HIV in poor countries by end of 2005; estimates 440,000 people are currently being treated, twice as many as in 2002, but falling short of goal of 500,000; attributes slow progress to lack of funding, though that has improved through contributions from Canada, Britain and Sweden; Stephen Lewis, United Nations envoy for AIDS in Africa, insists plan will succeed in Africa (M)

2004-07-11.title: In Their Own Words: Ahmed and a Protégé
2004-07-11.abstract: Following are Additional excerpts of Rabei Osman Sayed Ahmed's conversations that were intercepted and recorded by Italian law enforcement officials. Italian officials translated the transcripts from Arabic into Italian. The New York Times translated the Italian records into English.

2004-07-11.title: Voters in Japan Deal a Setback to Ruling Party
2004-07-11.abstract: Exit polls by Japanese television showed that Liberal Democrats and their allies won 48 of the 121 seats that were up for grabs, Bloomberg News reported.

2004-07-11.title: Iraq's Rebellion Develops Signs Of Internal Rift
2004-07-11.abstract: Tension appears to be rising between homegrown Iraqi resistance and foreign Islamist fighters who have entered Iraq to fight American military; experts believe this is reason why Iraq has not had spectacular attack for two weeks, even during transfer of formal sovereignty; two groups have been sniping at each other on Arabic television and Web sites, and in interviews with Iraqi and American officials; University of Baghdad Prof Dhary Rasheed warns that there is effort to push large resistance out of cities to minimize killing of innocent people, which internal rebels find abhorrent, and that sides are merely regrouping; split is welcome news to Prime Min Iyad Allawi, whose strategy is to appeal to patiotism of Iraqi fighters (M)

2004-07-12.title: An Exodus of African Nurses Puts Infants and the Ill in Peril
2004-07-12.abstract: Thousands of nurses in poorest countries in Africa, discouraged by low pay and grueling conditions, are leaving to take jobs in rich countries, primarily Britain; result of nursing crisis--neglect of the sick--is starkly apparent on wards of hospital in Malawi, where single nurse often looks after 50 or more desperately ill people while many former nurses there now minister to patients in private hospital rooms in Britain; African nurses are also migrating to United States and New Zealand, but in smaller numbers; human cost of nursing shortage in Malawi hospital described; because African governments paid to educate many health care workers who are leaving, African countries banded together at annual assembly of World Health Orgn in May to urge developed nations to compensate them for their lost investment; after intense debate, assembled countries resolved to search for ways to lessen damage of what they call increasing rates of emigration; photos; charts (L)

2004-07-12.title: Proponent of Democratic Change Takes Office as Serbian Leader
2004-07-12.abstract: Boris Tadic, leader of Democratic Party of Serbia and supporter of democratic changes, is sworn in as president, heralding hopes of new era; he defeated Tomislav Nikolic, far-right nationalist and onetime ally of Slobodan Milosevic; Tadic's victory gives new hope to those supporting change, even though powers of president are limited and his ability to initiate change depends largely on Parliament dominated by nationalists; photo (M)

2004-07-12.title: Bush's Pre-emptive Strategy Meets Some Untidy Reality
2004-07-12.abstract: Analysis: seering nature of report on intelligence failures leading up to war in Iraq is already fueling new debate over pre-emption, on campaign trial and among nations Pres Bush must convince as United States builds its case against North Korea and Iran; after Iraq, standard of proof has gotten higher (M)

2004-07-12.title: Iraq Struggles to Restore Its Endangered Tigris River
2004-07-12.abstract: Tigris River in Iraq has become smelly, shrunken, deserted, refuse-strewn ghost of its former splendor; Iraqi engineers, assisted by American environmental advocate and antiwar activist named Anna Bachmann, are trying to determine how to restore Tigris to some semblance of what it once was; photos; map (M)

2004-07-12.title: World Leaders Are Scarce as AIDS Conference Opens in Bangkok
2004-07-12.abstract: Summit meeting of world leaders invited to discuss AIDS is canceled at last minute as 15th International AIDS Conference begins, in Bangkok; record of national leaders attending and addressing past AIDS conferences has been inconsistent; United Nations Secretary General Kofi Annan says AIDS is not only health crisis, it is threat to development (M)

2004-07-13.title: Top Unit to Lead Investigation Of Journalist's Killing in Russia
2004-07-13.abstract: Russia offers assurances that apparent contract killing of crusading American journalist Paul Klebnikov will be handled by top prosecutorial unit and given highest priority; Klebnikov, who made his mark exposing malfeasance of group of extravagantly wealthy Russian oligarchs that emerged after collapse of Communism, was shot outside of his Forbes Russia magazine office; photo (M)

2004-07-13.title: Britain's Stiff Upper Lip Is Being Twisted Into a Snarl
2004-07-13.abstract: Britons appear to be abandoning ideal of stiff upper lip for which they were famous and adopting culture of complaint and litigation; some fear that people are losing their sense of personal responsibility and community spirit; photos (M)

2004-07-13.title: Russian Tycoon Again Offers to Give Up His Yukos Stock
2004-07-13.abstract: Russian tycoon Mikhail B Khodorkovsky returns to court, repeating offer to give up his shares in oil company Yukos even as government takes steps to collect more than $3.4 billion in back taxes; hearing focuses mostly on procedural issues, like denying motion for co-defendant Platon Lebedev to see a doctor, while students demonstrate in support of Khodorkovsky outside; photo (M)

2004-07-13.title: Empty Office Adds to Sense Of Isolation At the C.I.A.
2004-07-13.abstract: George J Tenet's departure as director of central intelligence leaves CIA officials to ponder agency's future in very new era; mood at agency turns dark and bitter, with many waiting for next shoe to drop; John E McLaughlin, new acting chief, takes Tenet's place at early morning Oval Office briefing for Pres Bush; leading senators urge Bush to move swiftly to name new intelligence chief, meaning that McLaughlin's tenure is likely to be brief; photo (M)

2004-07-13.title: Frenchwoman Says She Lied About Anti-Semitic Attack
2004-07-13.abstract: A woman whose report of an anti-Semitic attack had transfixed the nation admitted today that she had made up the story.

2004-07-14.title: Calm in Iraq Is Shattered by Baghdad Blast and Assassination
2004-07-14.abstract: The deadliest single attack since Iraq regained sovereignty was followed by the assassination of the governor of Mosul.

2004-07-14.title: Names of the Dead
2004-07-14.abstract: List of Americans newly confirmed dead in Iraq (S)

2004-07-14.title: Chechen Leader Escapes Separatist Bomb Attack
2004-07-14.abstract: Remotely detonated bomb strikes motorcade of Chechnya's acting President Sergei Abramov, who narrowly escapes injury, Grozny; kills presidential guard and injures three others; raises further qustions about Russia's stand that war in province is under control; follows extended skirmishes between Chechen special services soldiers loyal to Kremlin and militants, Avtury (M)

2004-07-14.title: As Britain Awaits Report, Blair Defends War Decision
2004-07-14.abstract: Prime Min Tony Blair defends his decision to send British troops to Iraq, news conference day before release of Lord Butler's potentially damaging report on Britain's pre-war intelligence; dismisses suggestion he was fed apocryphal data on weapons of mass destruction that made him look foolish around world; holds Saddam Hussein's ouster vindicated war; photo (M)

2004-07-14.title: Israel to Reroute Path of Barrier in West Bank
2004-07-14.abstract: Defense Ministry says Israel revises planned path of sections of West Bank separation barrier not yet built in effort to comply with Israeli court ruling that called for barrier to be less burdensome on Palestinians; says it is not reconsidering sections already built; Palestinians say they will continue to oppose all sections of barrier built inside West Bank; Israel is insistent that all changes be based on Israeli court ruling, not International Court of Justice ruling; Israel's opposition Labor Party agrees to take part in talks on joining Prime Min Ariel Sharon's coalition government; Sharon discusses barrier with senior American envoys Stephen Hadley and Elliott Abrams; Israel reports its troops shot to death local Islamic Jihad commander, Jenin; Palestinians identify man as Numan Tahaynah (M)

2004-07-15.title: In Chaos, Palestinians Struggle for a Way Out
2004-07-15.abstract: A People Adrift, first of two articles on Palestinians of West Bank and Gaza Strip after four years of Arab-Israeli conflict; contest is under way between warlords and democrats, between Islamists and secular leaders, between those who would destroy Israel and those who would live beside it; Israel's barrier against West Bank Palestinians is sign that after 37 years of occupying West Bank and Gaza, it seeks to cut itself off from Palestinians, to give up Gaza, to hold onto as much of West Bank as it can, and to retain Jewish majority in democratic state; for Palestinians there is no such clarity; they have made no national decisions, and mechanisms for making and enforcing any are breaking down; in Jenin, city of 45,000, many residents rely on themselves rather than Palestinian Authority; Mayor Waleed A Mwais blames Israel, saying it destroyed all forms of authority and now everyone has their own weapon; criticism of aging Palestinian leadership, even of Yasir Arafat, has reached new pitch; reform-minded leaders struggle to find way to start over; 3,200 Palestinians and 1,000 Israelis have died violently in conflict that has become way of life; photos; map (L)

2004-07-15.title: British Intelligence Report's Findings
2004-07-15.abstract: Highlights of report by Lord Butler on Britain's prewar intelligence used to justify war in Iraq (M)

2004-07-15.title: Governor of Iraqi Province Dies in Ambush
2004-07-15.abstract: Gov Osama Kashmoula of Nineveh, sprawling and ethnically diverse northern province of Iraq, is ambushed and killed on his way to Baghdad, dealing new setback to American-led rebuilding of country and providing stark illustration of how freely terrorists are choosing their targets; two of Kashmoula's bodyguards are also killed in attack; before becoming governor, he had been professor of agriculture at Mosul Univ; photo; map (M)

2004-07-15.title: On Bastille Day, France Buzzes Over a Hoax and Racism
2004-07-15.abstract: Pres Jacques Chirac, in traditional live television interview on Bastille Day, is forced to address event that has riveted France: account of anti-Semitic attack against young French mother and child on commuter train that has turned out to be hoax; Chirac suggests that woman will be punished, adding that her action reflects atmosphere of racism that has infected France; story continues to dominate front pages of newspapers, which begin to analyze why everyone from president and police to newspaper reporters got it so wrong; woman, Marie Leblanc, has history of filing complaints for assaults that were never proved; Chirac admits that country's effort to integrate people of different religions and ethnic backgrounds into fabric of French society is not succeeding (M)

2004-07-15.title: Acting Chief Insists Agencies Aren't at Fault in War Debate
2004-07-15.abstract: John E McLaughlin, new acting intelligence chief, says American intelligence agencies should not be blamed if there was inadequate debate about decision to go to war against Iraq; his comments are aimed at Senate Intelligence Committee, whose report portrays American intelligence agencies as having exaggerated evidence that Iraq had illicit weapons; comments also are implicit retort to arguments that CIA, not Pres Bush, was primarily responsible for sending country to war (M)

2004-07-16.title: C.I.A. Plays It Safe by Accentuating the Negative
2004-07-16.abstract: Some intelligence analysts see emphasizing a potential threat as a key to bureaucratic survival.

2004-07-16.title: More Killings in Iraq; Group Claims Governor's Assassination
2004-07-16.abstract: A group linked to a Jordanian terrorist suspect took credit for assassinating the governor of Nineveh province.

2004-07-16.title: Mandela Appeals to AIDS Conference to Extend Fight to TB
2004-07-16.abstract: Nelson Mandela attends 15th International AIDS Conference in Bangkok to lend his prestige to battles against tuberculosis and AIDS, deadly diseases that are intricately linked; he contracted tuberculosis during his 27 years in prison; he acknowledges now that as president he did not recognize severity of AIDS epidemic in South Africa, which now leads world with 5.3 million people infected with HIV; AIDS virus, by weakening immune system, leaves infected people particularly vulnerable to developing tuberculosis; Mandela says tuberculosis is ignored and he wants to help Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation open $44.7 million program for research into control of tuberculosis in communities where there are many cases of HIV; determining whether tuberculosis or HIV caused death of person with AIDS can be difficult and depends on specific medical facts in each case; photo (M)

2004-07-16.title: IRAQ UPDATE
2004-07-16.abstract: Recap of latest events in Iraq; map (S)

2004-07-16.title: Marine Who Had Disappeared Returns to U.S. for Questioning
2004-07-16.abstract: Marine Corps official says Cpl Wassef Ali Hassoun, who turned up in Beirut last week after vanishing in Iraq, has returned to United States for questioning and 'repatriation process' to assess his physical and mental condition; says Hassoun will be debriefed by psychologists and military intelligence officials; says he will have access to military lawyers and family members but his movements will be 'controlled'; Naval investigators looking into his disappearance are said to be looking at range of possibilities, including reports that he was kidnapped, deserted his unit or staged elaborate hoax with aid of Iraqis; photo (M)

2004-07-17.title: Names of the Dead
2004-07-17.abstract: Defense Department confirms deaths of three service members in Iraq

2004-07-17.title: U.N. Fund for Halting Diseases Faces Money Woes
2004-07-17.abstract: UN Global Fund executive director Richard G A Feachem says unexpectedly small flow of contributions from international donors has forced fund to impose six-month delay on new applications for money from organizations that fight AIDS, tuberculosis and malaria; says fund is seeking $3.5 billion for 2005 projects, but so far donors have pledged only $900 million (M)

2004-07-17.title: G.I.'s in Battle of Wits With Rebels Over Bomb Technology
2004-07-17.abstract: Iraqi insurgents, in deadly game of technological one-upmanship, have been adapting their most effective weapon, concealed and remotely detonated bomb, to increasingly sophisticated US attempts to detect devices before they explode; US soldiers say modifications suggest that there is technical elite among insurgents who are guiding changing designs; photos (M)

2004-07-17.title: Seminary Sex Scandal Divides Austrian Church
2004-07-17.abstract: Austrian news magazine publishes photographs of rector and deputy rector of Roman Catholic seminary in St Polten apparently kissing and fondling seminary students; publication of photographs comes after thousands of images downloaded from child pornography Web sites appeared on seminary computers, prompting police investigation; disclosures have taken on added importance because person in charge of St Polten Seminary is Bishop Kurt Krenn, prominent conservative; Krenn photo (M)

2004-07-17.title: Militants Say They Killed Iraqi Official In Ambush
2004-07-17.abstract: Several people are killed and dozens are wounded in sporadic violence across Iraq; group linked to Jordanian militant Abu Musab al-Zarqawi takes responsibility for killing of Nineveh Province Gov Osama Kashmoula (M)

2004-07-18.title: Editor's Death Raises Questions About Change in Russia
2004-07-18.abstract: Profile of Forbes Russia editor Paul Klebnikov, who was murdered on July 9; Klebnikov arrived in Russia with a spirit of civic reform and dreamed of a better Russia; was optimistic that Russia was settling down after turmoil of 1990's, but had growing concern about government power; few believe Klebnikov's killer will be found; his murder raises questions about prospects for change in Russia; photo (M)

2004-07-18.title: U.S. Diplomat Starts New Job By Deferring To the Iraqis
2004-07-18.abstract: US Ambassador to Iraq John D Negroponte, speaking for first time in Baghdad, draws distinction between new American diplomatic presence in Iraq and former Coalition Provisional Authority; emphasizes that his role will be to assist new Iraqi government; Negroponte's goals include training Iraqi soldiers to assume security responsibilities, helping government hold elections in January 2005 and pushing ahead on reconstruction projects that have been hindered by sabotage (M)

2004-07-18.title: With Win in Sights, Armstrong Settles for 2nd
2004-07-18.abstract: Lance Armstrong left high altitude in second place overall with a nearly clear path to his sixth consecutive Tour victory.

2004-07-18.title: In Slow, Measured Steps, Iraqis Take Their Places in the Ranks of Security Forces
2004-07-18.abstract: Newly-trained Iraqi troops are slowly replacing American counterparts in public eye as new Iraqi government struggles to prove its sovereignty; government hopes Iraqi troops will be more effective at gathering intelligence and be less provocative presence than American troops, although Americans still direct operations; opinion polls show many Iraqis would like American troops gone, but most would not feel safe without them; photos (M)

2004-07-18.title: Despite Appeals, Chaos Still Stalks the Sudanese
2004-07-18.abstract: Conditions are still miserable in and around Nyala, Sudan, two weeks after Sec of State Colin L Powell and UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan visited that part of Sudan in hope that glare of diplomatic shame might arrest human crisis; days after diplomats end their tour, gunmen storm girls' school in desert region of Darfur, chain group of students together and set building on fire; charred remains of eight girls are still in shackles when military observers from African Union arrive on scene; Sudanese government promised diplomats it would rein in Janjaweed militias, but killing and raping continue, and health conditions are more dangerous; photos; map (L)

2004-07-19.title: Arafat Struggles to Resolve Leadership Crisis
2004-07-19.abstract: Yasir Arafat is facing perhaps the strongest internal challenge since establishing the Palestinian government a decade ago.

2004-07-19.title: Fear of Betrayal
2004-07-19.abstract: The Kurds suspect that Baghdad is trying to force them onto the sidelines, and are threatening to secede. They have apparently found an ally in Israel, of all countries.

2004-07-19.title: IRAQ GIVES ORDER TO REOPEN PAPER G.I.'S HAD CLOSED
2004-07-19.abstract: Prime Min Iyad Allawi makes conciliatory gesture to Moktada al-Sadr, ordering reopening of his radical Shiite newspaper Al Hawza, and approves American airstrike meant to pound insurgent fighters in Falluja who have been attacking American and Iraqi forces there; American soldiers' closing of Al Hawza on orders of American administrator L Paul Bremer III four months ago was catalyst for some of worst anti-American mayhem of occupation; Allawi's two actions seen as early evidence of his stated strategy for taming deadly insurgency by making concessions to fighters who cooperate and cracking down on those who do not; although American officials say airstrike in Falluja was carried out after he endorsed it, it is unclear how much influence he has with American military; he has had to walk fine line between toughness and compromise; many Iraqis, expressing outrage and humiliation at being occupied, supported attacks against American forces; Allawi is offering amnesty to fighters who did not commit serious crimes, even as he promises hard action against those who do not cooperate; photos (M)

2004-07-19.title: IRAQ UPDATE
2004-07-19.abstract: Brief recap of latest events in Iraq; map (S)

2004-07-19.title: David Blow, 72; Briton Developed Method to Study Proteins
2004-07-19.abstract: David Blow, British biophysicist who helped develop X-ray crystallography, technique used to unravel molecular structures of animal and human proteins, dies at age 72 (S)

2004-07-20.title: Politicus : A French son's angst over Nazi collaboration
2004-07-20.abstract: PARIS—  An 82-year-old man writes a book about his mother two decades after her death. "Lisette de Brinon, Ma Mère" serves as the title, but the book, a small marvel of melancholy frankness and restraint, is as much about a great awfulness that does not go away as the life of an unlovable Frenchwoman.

2004-07-20.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Inquiry Into Mislaid Terror Papers
2004-07-20.abstract: British authorities begin investigation into how secret antiterrorism documents about security features and weaknesses at Heathrow Airport were mislaid and then discovered by motorist on roadway near airport (S)

2004-07-20.title: Marine Who Was Missing for 3 Weeks Denies He Deserted in Iraq
2004-07-20.abstract: Cpl Wassef Ali Hassoun, marine who vanished last month in Iraq and turned up three weeks later in Lebanon, emphatically denies that he deserted; says he was captured and held against his will by anticoalition forces for 19 days; military is conducting investigation into his disappearance and resurfacing; he is now at military base at Quantico, Va, undergoing repatriation, routine process for any marine who has been captured or detained; photo (M)

2004-07-20.title: U.N. Opposes Israeli Barrier
2004-07-20.abstract: The General Assembly approved a nonbinding resolution demanding that Israel obey a World Court ruling that it dismantle its separation barrier on the West Bank.

2004-07-20.title: Governor and Defense Aide Assassinated in 2 Shootings
2004-07-20.abstract: Basra's interim governor is fatally shot by unknown assailants on street; his assassination follows confirmation by government that Defense Ministry official was assassinated days ago; deaths from suicide bombing continue as well; Iraqi soldiers and police officers are taking heaviest hits in recent revival of attacks aimed at Iraqi government institutions accused of collaborating with Americans there; insurgency is continuing unabated, even after Prime Min Iyad Allawi announces tough new emergency law; photos (M)

2004-07-21.title: Britain Says It Will Cut Armed Forces, but Modernize Them
2004-07-21.abstract: The cuts, trimming at least 15,000 people, would not affect Britain's commitment to maintaining 9,000 soldiers in Iraq.

2004-07-21.title: World Briefing | Americas: Canada: New Cabinet
2004-07-21.abstract: Canadian Prime Minister Paul Martin appoints new cabinet, which includes Pierre S Pettigrew as foreign minister, Bill Graham as defense minister and former hockey Hall-of-famer Ken Dryden, who is minister of social development; photo (S)

2004-07-21.title: Vatican to Investigate Pornography Accusations
2004-07-21.abstract: Pope John Paul II to name Austrian Bishop Klaus Kung to investigate charges of homosexual behavior and downloading of child pornography at St Polten Roman Catholic seminary in Austria; Kung photo; St Polten Bishop Kurt Krenn, conservative who is intensely disliked by reformist Catholics, refuses to resign in scandal (S)

2004-07-21.title: Hostage Is Freed After Philippine Troops Are Withdrawn From Iraq
2004-07-21.abstract: Filipino truck driver is released in Baghdad after Philippines removes last of its 51 soldiers and police officers from Iraq month early, as kidnappers demanded; US, Australia and Iraqi government criticize decision, but Prime Min Gloria Macapagal Arroyo says it would have been silly to sacrifice Angelo dela Cruz's life when troops were leaving anyway; four Iraqi veterinarians are killed, three wounded when bomb hits minibus in Baquba; men dressed in police uniforms are linked to assassination of interim governor, Hazem Tawfeek al-Ainichi, in Basra (M)

2004-07-21.title: Group Accuses Pakistan Fighters of Killing and Torturing Farmers
2004-07-21.abstract: Human Rights Watch charges that Pakistani paramilitary forces have killed at least four Pakistani farmers, tortured dozens of others and arrested hundreds more in violent dispute with tenant farmers over ownership of fertile land controlled by army in Punjab (S)

2004-07-22.title: World Briefing | Africa: Sudan: Militia Attacks Continue
2004-07-22.abstract: Jan Pronk, United Nations Secretary General Kofi Annan's special representative, reports that although Sudanese government is now allowing humanitarian aid to reach troubled Darfur region, attacks on Africans by government backed militias are continuing; John C Danforth, American ambassador to UN, urges Security Council resolution on conflict (S)

2004-07-22.title: War Costs Exceed Budget, Watchdog Panel Says
2004-07-22.abstract: General Accountability Office, formerly General Accounting Office, says military operations in Iraq and Afghanistan are running $12.3 billion over budget this year, and Pentagon officials are trying to make up for shortfall by transferring money from other accounts and delaying refurbishment of worn-out equipment in Iraq; Pentagon officials refuse to comment on accuracy of GAO estimate, but Bush administration officials have acknowledged that costs in Iraq are running higher than $65 billion that Congress approved for military operations in Iraq and Afghanistan; Democrats seize on GAO report, attacking administration for consistently underestimating war costs (M)

2004-07-22.title: Gay Pair Seeks Canada's First Same-Sex Divorce
2004-07-22.abstract: Two Toronto women who were among first same-sex couples to marry in Canada are now seeking what may be first Canadian same-sex divorce; couple had been together for nearly 10 years, but separated after five days of marriage; women are seeking to change Canada's divorce law, which still applies only to marriages between man and woman (M)

2004-07-22.title: Iraq Asks Aid In Keeping Out Infiltrators
2004-07-22.abstract: Iraqi Foreign Min Hoshyar Zebari, at meeting of foreign ministers from region, demands that its neighbors do more to halt any infiltration of foreign fighters across their borders; notes that repeated statements of support have not been translated into concrete steps; says foreign fighters provide suicide bombers responsible for some of worst attacks (S)

2004-07-22.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Mourning For Mine Deaths
2004-07-22.abstract: Ukrainian Prime Minister Viktor Yanukovych issues pledge to help families of at least 31 miners who died in methane gas explosion at coal mine in Donetsk region; five miners remain missing (S)

2004-07-23.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Diana's Ex-Lover Freed After Drug Arrest
2004-07-23.abstract: James Hewitt, former lover of Diana, Princess of Wales, and author of book about relationship, is arrested on alleged possession of Class A drug and faces possible prison sentence of up to seven years; photo (S)

2004-07-23.title: Names of the Dead
2004-07-23.abstract: Defense Dept confirms death of service member in Iraq (S)

2004-07-23.title: The Next Hard Step
2004-07-23.abstract: News Analysis: partisan wrangling and capital's entrenched resistance to change can be expected to impede adoption of costly, complicated steps 9/11 commission believes are needed to make country safer; there is reason to doubt whether government, let alone public, has fully embraced uncertain realities that have become 'new normal' in aftermath of 9/11; ports, power grids, water supplies, rail hubs and many private buildings remain vulnerable in society that remains open by its essence; some initial political and bureaucratic response to commission's findings and recommendations sound suspiciously like business as usual; while Pres Bush himself has praised report, top White House officials remain noncommittal on specifics; panel's call for new national intelligence director is already being opposed by Pentagon, CIA, Homeland Security Sec Tom Ridge and some in Congress (M)

2004-07-23.title: New Details in Battle of Hijackers and Passengers to Control Plane
2004-07-23.abstract: Report from 9/11 commission provides new, chilling details about what happened in cockpit of United Airlines flight 93 in its last minutes; it is gripping account of battle to gain control of aircraft by passengers who knew that terrorists had seized plane and were determined to prevent them from using it as missile; recounts how hijackers struggled to maintain control as passengers sought to storm cockpit door; says hijackers intentionally crashed plane into field in Shanksville, Pa, when it appeared they might be overcome by passengers; photo (M)

2004-07-23.title: Report Cites Lapses Across Government And 2 Presidencies
2004-07-23.abstract: Bipartisan 9/11 commission says in final report that Clinton and Bush administrations failed to grasp gravity of threat from Al Qaeda before 9/11 attacks and left counterterrorism efforts to disparate collection of uncoordinated, underfinanced and dysfunctional government agencies; report claims that across government, there were 'failures of imagination, policy, capabilities and management'; chronicles agonizingly slow recognition by Clinton and Bush administrations of danger that Osama bin Laden's Al Qaeda network represented; describes in detail how 9/11 plot, despite setbacks, gathered shape and direction; says when terrorists struck, national security experts were still pondering counterterrorism strategy and agencies responsible for protecting country were completely blindsided; report does not address directly whether attacks might have been prevented; many of its findings have been foreshadowed in 17 staff reports previously made public, but final report does contain some new information, including detailed account of how passengers on Flight 93 tried to gain control of their hijacked plane; photo (M)

2004-07-24.title: Despite pullout from Iraq, Philippine leader reaffirms support of U.S. and allies in 'fight' : Arroyo stands by her call
2004-07-24.abstract: MANILA:—  President Gloria Macapagal Arroyo on Friday sought to contain the damage created by her concession to Iraqi insurgents, reassuring the United States that she remains a strong ally of the Bush administration.

2004-07-24.title: U.S. Lays Out Case in British Court to Extradite Cleric
2004-07-24.abstract: Lawyers acting on behalf of US government in London seek extradition of Abu Hamza al-Masri, Egyptian-born Muslim cleric accused of supporting Al Qaeda; Masri was arrested in London in May; faces charges of hostage-taking and conspiracy in connection with attack in Yemen in 1998 on 16 tourists (M)

2004-07-24.title: Report details Qaeda's skill in faking passports
2004-07-24.abstract: WASHINGTON:—  At times verging on a how-to guide for sneaking across international borders, the newly released report of the Sept. 11 commission details the ease with which terrorists obtained, faked and doctored passports to hoodwink immigration services around the world.

2004-07-24.title: Names of the Dead
2004-07-24.abstract: Defense Department confirms deaths of three service members in Iraq (S)

2004-07-24.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Putin Orders 'Stalingrad' On Memorial
2004-07-24.abstract: Russian President Vladimir V Putin orders that name Volgograd be replaced by Stalingrad at Tomb of Unknown Soldier near Kremlin Wall in commemoration of 60th anniversary of end of World War II; battle of Stanlingrad is recognized as major turning point in war (S)

2004-07-25.title: Brazil Carries the War on Drugs to the Air
2004-07-25.abstract: Brazil announces it will shoot down aircraft trafficking in illegal drugs; law permitting such action was passed in 1998, but policy was never put into effect because of United States' concern that accidental downing of civilian planes could expose American government and companies to lawsuits; Brazil's northern Amazon cooridor has increasingly become essential smuggling route; Brazil tracked 4,128 'unauthorized flights' in 2003; new policy will go into effect in late October; photos; map (M)

2004-07-25.title: Karzai Puts Off Visit to Pakistan
2004-07-25.abstract: President Hamid Karzai of Afghanistan postponed a state visit to Pakistan at the last minute amid political tension at home.

2004-07-25.title: IRAQ UPDATE
2004-07-25.abstract: Update of events in Iraq; map (S)

2004-07-25.title: North Korea Seems to Reject Butter-for-Guns Proposal From U.S.
2004-07-25.abstract: North Korea appears to reject United States offer for gradual lifting of sanctions and for international aid in exchange for rapid dismantlement of nuclear weapons program; dismisses proposal as 'sham offer' because its requires North Korea to disarm and submit to inspections before it receives full benefits of economic concessions; North Korea has offered only to return to 'freeze' in weapons program (M)

2004-07-25.title: Pakistan Choreographs Vote for Chosen Premier
2004-07-25.abstract: Critics charge that Pakistani Pres Pervez Musharraf is choreographing election of Shaukat Aziz, his chosen premier; Aziz is finance minister, but is required to be member of Parliament in order to serve as prime minister; Aziz will run in carefully chosen, pro-government district that is being showered with government money; personifies moderate technocrat image Musharraf wants to convey for country; choice of Aziz reflects Musharraf's view that generals and technocrats can do better job of running country than old-line corrupt politicians; photo (M)

2004-07-26.title: Dispute Prompts Afghan Leader to Delay Trip
2004-07-26.abstract: Pres Hamid Karzai of Afghanistan postpones state visit to Pakistan amid political tension at home with his Defense Minister Marshal Muhammad Qasim Fahim; at issue is Fahim's reluctance to resign immediately from his positions as vice president and defense minister if, as expected, Hamid picks him as one of two running mates for vice president in Oct 9 presidential election; generals from Northern Alliance faction loyal to Fahim say they were called to Kabul and put on alert; rumors circulate that American troops are going to take control of ancient fortress that commands heights of Kabul, where Fahim retains few remaining heavy weapons and tanks in city; photo (M)

2004-07-26.title: Iran Acquittal In Death Case Is Challenged
2004-07-26.abstract: Lawyer representing family of slain Iranian-Canadian photojournalist Zahra Kazemi vows to continue struggle to bring her killer to justice, one day after court in Tehran clears Iranian intelligence agent Muhammad Reza Aghdam Ahmadi of responsibility in her death; she died of brain hemorrage in July 2003 while in detention for having taken photographs outside prison in Tehran (M)

2004-07-26.title: Names of the Dead
2004-07-26.abstract: Defense Dept confirms death of service member in Iraq (S)

2004-07-26.title: Encounter with the Messiah
2004-07-26.abstract: Things are getting serious for President Bush: If his challenger Kerry succeeds in making a convincing appearance at the Democratic Convention in Boston, Bush may lose the election in the fall.

2004-07-26.title: Stepped-Up Police Activity Irks an Arab Area in Greece
2004-07-26.abstract: Greek, American and British intelligence agencies increase their activities in predominantly Muslim district of Athens, Greece, before opening of Olympic Games, angering residents there; Greek officials say they asked for, and received, assistance from counterterrorism teams and intelligence operatives from Jordan and Egypt; says Israelis are keeping eye on Arab embassies; say 100,000 people of Arab or Muslim background who live in Athens have generally not been militant, but fear is that Al Qaeda or affiliated terrorist group has planted sleeper cell in their neighborhood; Col Muammar el-Qaddafi of Libya sends word to Libyan population in Athens not to engage in acts of violence during Games, and Palestinians make similar appeals to their residents in city; photo (M)

2004-07-27.title: U.S. Hands Over Four French Terror Suspects
2004-07-27.abstract: The four men were immediately detained by French authorities under the country's tough anti-terrorism laws.

2004-07-27.title: World Briefing | Europe: Italy: 400 Migrants Land In 3 Days
2004-07-27.abstract: More than 400 illegal immigrants trying to reach Italy land on tiny Sicilian island of Lampedusa, where they are detained (S)

2004-07-27.title: U.S. Sees No Basis to Prosecute Iranian Opposition 'Terror' Group Being Held in Iraq
2004-07-27.abstract: Senior American officials report that 16-month review has found no basis to charge members of Iranian opposition group in Iraq with violations of American law, though group, People's Mujahedeen of Iran, is listed as terrorist organization by US government; group's camp was bombed by US military in Apr 2003; about 3,800 members of group are being held in de facto American custody in prison camp 60 miles from Baghdad; although US does not appear to have evidence to charge individual members of group with acts of terrorism, it also appears unwilling to surrender its members to their enemy, Iran; some opponents of Iran argue that People's Mujahedeen serves as effective source of pressure on Iranian government and should be rewarded, not punished, by US (M)

2004-07-27.title: Early Steps, Maybe, Toward a Democracy in Iraq
2004-07-27.abstract: Experiment in democracy in Iraq is at once inspiring and troubled, full of potential but not at all assured of success; caucuses are convening around country to select 1,000 delegates, who will hold national conference in Baghdad in next week with goal of voting--openly and freely--on 100-seat transitional council that will oversee government of interim Prime Minister Iyad Allawi until national elections are held in Jan; conference is also meant to function as opportunity for national dialogue, in which for first time since fall of Saddam Hussein, Iraqis from all religions, regions and political and ethnic groups begin to discuss way forward; because of widespread violence and fragmentation, United Nations is urging Iraq to postpone conference at least briefly; fear is that thousand or more fledgling politicians would make tempting terror target; major issue is that many groups considered crucial to any broad national dialogue are refusing to take part, largely because they view process as controlled by United States; photo; chart (M)

2004-07-28.title: World Briefing | Americas: Haiti: Spain and Morocco To Send Joint Mission
2004-07-28.abstract: Morocco and Spain agree to send peacekeeping mission to Haiti; it is first time two nations have combined efforts and formed single military unit (S)

2004-07-28.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Dying Surname
2004-07-28.abstract: Seventy-three-year-old Briton Mike Pimbury, appearing on BBC, appeals for anyone with same surname to contact him; has no children and wants surname to live on (S)

2004-07-28.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Video Of Raiding Warlord
2004-07-28.abstract: Chechen leader Shamil Basayev appears in video on pro-rebel Web site claiming responsibility for recent Ingushetia raids in which almost 100 people were killed; Basayev has been linked to Al Qaeda (S)

2004-07-28.title: World Bank Challenged: Are the Poor Really Helped?
2004-07-28.abstract: Development economists question effectiveness of huge sums spent in third world each year by wealthy nations and international organizations like World Bank, saying there is scant evidence that myriad projects have made any real difference in lives of world's 2.7 billion poor people; MIT's Poverty Action Lab, in randomized evaluations that are considered gold standard of policy research, finds good results from simple tactics, such as hiring remedial tutors in Bombay slum or giving Kenyan students free uniforms and breakfast, and even better results from deworming drugs that make children healthy enough to go to school; photos (M)

2004-07-29.title: Durban Journal; As AIDS Continues to Ravage, South Africa 'Recycles' Graves
2004-07-29.abstract: AIDS pandemic is so sweeping in Durban, South Africa, that people are dying faster than city can find space to bury them; gravediggers are reopening existing graves and interring fresh bones atop old bones; 51 of city's 53 municipal cemeteries are officially filled to capacity; official who heads city's dept of parks and cemeteries fears that all of Durban will become one big graveyard if epidemic continues; roughly one in eight South Africans is HIV-positive; in Durban, country's third-largest city, 35 percent of pregnant women surveyed two years ago were infected with HIV; Durban held conference on cemetery problem and discovered that host of other graveyards--in Cape Town, Bloemfontein, Pretoria, Port Elizabeth--are also filling up at alarming rates; photos; map (M)

2004-07-29.title: Convention Notebook : Outside the convention, Howard Dean still a star
2004-07-29.abstract: CORRECTION PUBLISHED IN THE INTERNATIONAL HERALD TRIBUNE DATED

2004-07-29.title: At Colombia's Congress, Paramilitary Chiefs Talk Peace
2004-07-29.abstract: Three top commanders of Colombia's right-wing death squads emerge from their government-granted haven in north to speak in Bogota before country's Congress, professing firm commitment to fragile peace talks aimed at disarming their 15,000-member paramiltary force; three, Salvatore Mancuso, Ramon Isaza and Ivan Roberto Duque, commanders of United Self-Defense Forces of Columbia, were invited to speak by members of congress who claim paramilitary group is misunderstood; three are eager to alter their reputations as mass killers and criminals; their appearance infuriates those who want justice for thousands of Colombians killed by paramiltaries in their long war to erode support for Marxist rebels; photos (M)

2004-07-29.title: Ex-Official Challenged on Madrid Bomb Inquiry
2004-07-29.abstract: Former Interior Min Angel Acebes of Spain comes under fierce questioning in Congress about why he had continued to say domestic Basque terrorists were primary suspects in Mar 11 train bombings, even after police said they were not; claims that when he gave news conference on Mar 13 citing ETA as main suspect, police had not yet determined that evidence pointed decisively to Islamic terrorists; Acebes's party was defeated in elections three days after attacks; photo (M)

2004-07-30.title: U.S. Alters Sudan Resolution to Attract U.N. Votes
2004-07-30.abstract: United States modifies wording of Security Council resolution on Sudan to try to win over countries reluctant to endorse threat of sanctions against Sudanese government; vote on measure is scheduled for July 30; resolution is aimed at forcing Sudan to disarm and bring to justice Arab militias armed by government that are responsible for deaths of at least 30,000 black Africans and forced displacement of more than million (M)

2004-07-30.title: North Korea Denounces Seoul for Welcoming Defectors
2004-07-30.abstract: North Korea denounces South Korean government for granting asylum to nearly 460 North Korean defectors, largest such group to arrive in South Korea; defectors are thought to have come through Vietnam; size of group results from increasing popularity of smuggling route out of China, not from increase in people fleeing North Korea; China has recently tightened security on its border with North Korea; latest defectors were brought to Seoul in top-secret operation of South Korean government, which feared antagonizing North, as well as other countries through which defectors had traveled; photo (M)

2004-07-30.title: Hague War Crimes Tribunal Frees a Convicted General
2004-07-30.abstract: Appeals court of war crimes tribunal in The Hague throws out conviction on major charges of Tihomir Blaskic, former Bosnian Croat general, and reduces his sentence to 9 years from 45 years; he will become most senior offical to be released, after spending more than eight years in tribunal cell; new ruling throws out indictment charging him with crimes against humanity and war crimes against Muslims in Bosnia in 1993; appeals panel claims lower court misinterpreted law, made factual errors, obtained insufficient evidence and meted out unfair punishment; several British and Balkan military officers testified in favor of Blaskic; photo (M)

2004-07-30.title: In Pakistan, Turning Grief Into New Political Muscle
2004-07-30.abstract: News analysis of reaction in Pakistan to news that two Pakistani workers who had been kidnapped by Islamic militants in Iraq were beheaded; some American officials thought killing of innocent Pakistanis by terrorists might increase support for American effort to stabilize Iraq; instead, there is widespread anger among Pakistanis at their government, and Pres Pervez Musharraf is being urged not to dispatch troops to Iraq; Pakistanis condemn terrorists' tactics, but contend that they see Iraq as American problem, not a Pakistani one (M)

2004-07-30.title: Powell Tries to Reassure Iraqis in Surprise Visit
2004-07-30.abstract: Secretary of State Colin S. Powell told anxious and embittered Iraqis that the U.S. would stand by its pledge to bring "peace, freedom and democracy" to their country.

2004-07-31.title: Pakistan Official Unhurt as 5 Die in Attack
2004-07-31.abstract: A suicide bomber blew himself up next to the car of Pakistan's prime minister-designate, Shaukat Aziz, at a crowded political rally on Friday evening, killing the driver and four other people but leaving Mr. Aziz unhurt, according to the police and witnesses.     The attack, near this northwestern town in Punjab Province, also wounded 45 people, including seven police officials, the police said.

2004-07-31.title: NATO Ready To Help Train Iraqi Forces
2004-07-31.abstract: NATO overcame French opposition and agreed Friday to dispatch ''as soon as possible'' a small group of officers to Iraq to train the country's military.     The agreement was an important political milestone for the alliance but in military terms represented only a modest contribution to the efforts to stabilize Iraq. NATO is to send fewer than 40 officers, the secretary general, Jaap de Hoop Scheffer, said at a news conference, but he called the mission a ''nucleus'' of future operations.

2004-07-31.title: At Least 2 Are Killed in Coordinated Blasts at Embassies in Uzbekistan
2004-07-31.abstract: Coordinated bomb attacks struck the perimeters of the American and Israeli embassies on Friday in Tashkent, the capital of Uzbekistan, killing at least two people and wounding several more, according to government officials and news reports.     A third explosion at about the same time hit the lobby of Uzbekistan's general prosecutor's office.

2004-07-31.title: Bush Faces New Obstacles In Keeping Allies' Support
2004-07-31.abstract: With the demands of the presidential campaign adding new urgency to the search for a strategy to exit Iraq, American officials are facing many complications in holding together the coalition of countries they persuaded to support the war.     The kidnappings and executions of foreign workers, and the decision by some companies and several countries to leave the American-led forces, have compounded the burdens on the 140,000 American troops there and the diplomatic pressures on Bush administration officials intent on staying the course.

2004-07-31.title: Globalist : Kerry tries to reassure America and the world
2004-07-31.abstract: BOSTON—  'I'm John Kerry and I'm reporting for duty." With that little sentence, and the brisk salute of a man who saw war in Vietnam, Kerry stepped up to the podium to accept the Democratic nomination for president. The words carried a double message.
